item: Custom Icon System vs Library Selection
definition_origin: An icon system is the curated, consistent set of UI icons used across a product — for navigation, actions, status, and metadata. Origins: pictograms in early Macintosh (Susan Kare, 1984), evolved through Windows Vista's full-color treatments, the Material Design 1 monochrome filled grid (Google, 2014), Apple's SF Symbols system (2019), and the open-source explosion 2018-2024 (Heroicons by Tailwind Labs, Phosphor by the Phosphor team, Lucide as a community fork of Feather, Tabler, Radix, Iconoir). 2026 reality: the choice is no longer 'which library' — it's the strategic question of LIBRARY vs CUSTOM vs HYBRID, plus the deeper realization that for some products, NUMERICAL HIERARCHY (01., 02., 03.) replaces icons entirely.
core_purpose: **Visceral**: icons are the alphabet of the interface — when consistent in stroke, weight, and metaphor, they read as quietly professional; when mixed, the entire UI feels amateur. **Behavioral**: icons compress vocabulary — a magnifying glass is faster to recognize than the word 'search' across cultures. They become anti-patterns when ambiguous (the hamburger menu argument). **Reflective**: icon system is one of three brand visual fingerprints (the others being typography and color). Linear's hand-drawn-feeling icon set IS part of why Linear feels different. The 2026 trend: many premium products skip icons entirely in nav (Notion, Things, Vercel dashboard) — using numerical hierarchy + typography because 'no icon' is an aesthetic statement that signals confidence. For our user's Magician/Creator + Caregiver mix: Phosphor's Duotone weight is uniquely on-archetype (warm + crafted); Lucide's stroke-based default reads more Hero/Sage; Heroicons reads as 'I used Tailwind defaults' (read: lazy). Custom icons signal Magician/Creator most strongly but cost the most.
when_needed: **Mandatory**: any app with a tab bar, action toolbar, or list-row meta needs at least 20-50 icons. **Library is fine** for B2B internal tools, MVP consumer apps, AI-wrapper products where the icon system is not the differentiator. **Custom needed** for: brand-driven consumer apps where the icon system carries identity (Linear, Vercel, Stripe, Things, Apple's own apps); when archetype is Magician/Creator and the rest of the brand is restrained (icons become the only place to express creativity); when no library matches your scale/stroke need. **Hybrid** is most common: pick one library for 80% of utility icons, custom for 5-15 hero icons (your tab bar, your brand-defining actions). Numerical hierarchy approach: viable for editorial apps, settings menus, onboarding lists where 8+ items need ordering and an icon would feel forced.
format_options: **SVG** is the universal source — 1-3KB per icon after SVGO. **Icon font** (older approach, 2010-2018 era) — ship one TTF/WOFF2 with hundreds of glyphs, address by Unicode point; smaller HTTP overhead but worse for color/animation/accessibility; in 2026 mostly deprecated. **React component** — `<SearchIcon size={20} stroke={1.5}/>`; Lucide, Phosphor, Heroicons all ship as tree-shakeable React/RN components — best DX. **Sprite sheet** — single SVG with `<symbol>` definitions, referenced by `<use href='#search'/>` — efficient when you ship 100+ icons but worse tree-shaking. **SF Symbols** (iOS-only) — Apple's variable-weight icon font, free, 5000+ glyphs, supports multi-weight/multi-color/animation natively; SwiftUI/UIKit only, not in React Native unless you bridge or use names like `expo-symbols`. **Material Symbols** (Google variable font) — Android equivalent, similar capability. Pros/cons: React component = best for Web/RN apps; Icon font = legacy compatibility only; Sprite sheet = OK for static sites; SF/Material Symbols = best per-platform native fidelity but breaks cross-platform consistency. **Recommendation**: React component import (Lucide or Phosphor) for cross-platform RN/Next.js codebase.
file_size_performance: Single SVG icon: 1-3KB raw, 0.5-1.5KB after SVGO. Icon library tree-shaken (Lucide React, Phosphor React) imports only used icons — a typical app with 40 icons adds 30-80KB to bundle. Material Symbols variable font: 100-300KB for a subset, 1MB+ for full. SF Symbols: zero runtime cost (system-provided on iOS). Custom icon set of 60: deliver as ESM tree-shaken or SVGR-generated React components, total bundle impact ~50-150KB.
free_tools: 1. **Lucide** (FREE, MIT, https://lucide.dev) — 1500+ icons, stroke-based, consistent 24x24 grid, customizable strokeWidth (0.5-2.5). The shadcn/ui default. Best for Hero/Sage archetypes; clean and quietly precise. 2. **Phosphor Icons** (FREE, MIT, https://phosphoricons.com) — 9000+ icons in 6 weights (Thin/Light/Regular/Bold/Fill/Duotone). Best for Caregiver/Creator/Magician archetypes — duotone has unique warmth no other library matches. 3. **Heroicons** (FREE, MIT, https://heroicons.com) — 316 icons by Tailwind Labs, two styles (24px outline, 24px solid, 20px mini); good but limited and very recognizable as 'Tailwind defaults' — reads generic. 4. **Tabler Icons** (FREE, MIT, https://tabler.io/icons) — 5800+ icons, stroke-based, alternative to Lucide. 5. **Radix Icons** (FREE, MIT) — 300 utility icons by the Radix team; best for very minimal utility-focused B2B. 6. **Iconoir** (FREE, MIT, https://iconoir.com) — 1500+ stroke icons, slightly more idiosyncratic than Lucide. 7. **Material Symbols** (FREE, Apache, https://fonts.google.com/icons) — 3500+ variable font icons by Google, covers fill/weight/grade/optical-size axes. 8. **SF Symbols 6** (FREE, Apple license, iOS/macOS only) — 6000+ glyphs. 9. **Figma Free** — for authoring custom icons on a 24x24 pixel grid. 10. **Recraft Free (50 cred/day)** — generates custom icons as native SVG; the free AI tool best suited to icon work. 11. **SVGOMG** (FREE web, https://jakearchibald.github.io/svgomg/) — interactive SVGO for compressing finished icons. 12. **SVGR** (FREE CLI) — converts SVG to React/RN components.
paid_tools: **Starter ($10-15/mo)**: Recraft Basic ($12/mo) for AI-assisted custom icon generation in SVG. **Pro ($20-30/mo)**: Phosphor Pro Family ($16, one-time) — bonus weights and brand icons; rarely needed since free Phosphor covers 95% of cases. Streamline Icons ($249-499/yr) — 30K+ icons across consistent styles; only justified for design systems shipping massive scale. Adobe Illustrator ($22.99/mo) — heavy for icon work; Figma free is enough. **Enterprise ($50+)**: Iconscout subscription (~$24/mo); commission a freelance icon designer for full custom set ($1500-5000 one-time, on Dribbble) — only justified if icons are core to brand identity. **Verdict for our user**: free Phosphor + free Lucide + Recraft free + Figma free covers everything. Custom set commission only after PMF and only if icons are a brand differentiator.
gemini_pro_workflow:
**Step 1 — System spec writing (Gemini 2.5 Pro chat)**: 'Help me design an icon system spec. App is {description}. Choose: stroke weight (1.25/1.5/1.75/2), corner radius (0/2/4 px), grid size (16/20/24), perspective (orthographic/3-quarter), level of detail (low/medium/high). Reference systems I admire: {Linear, Phosphor Duotone, Phosphor Bold, custom Stripe icons}. Output a one-page constraint doc.'

**Step 2 — Library audit**: Use NotebookLM. Drop in Phosphor.json + Lucide.json (icon name lists). Ask 'For my app surfaces {list 30 needed icons}, which icons does each library cover and which need custom?' Output a Phosphor-vs-Lucide-vs-Custom decision matrix.

**Step 3 — Custom icon generation with Imagen 4 (only for the gap icons)**: Prompt: 'Single UI icon, 24x24 pixel grid, stroke weight 1.5px, rounded line caps, single color (black on transparent), depicting {concept}, geometric simplicity matching {reference image of Phosphor Bold}, no decorative flourishes, no gradient, centered on canvas with 2px breathing room.' Generate 10-20, cull to 1-2 candidates per slot.

**Step 4 — Recraft for SVG output**: Take the best Imagen raster and prompt Recraft 'redraw as flat vector SVG, single 1.5px stroke, no fill, 24x24'. Get editable SVG.

**Step 5 — Figma cleanup**: Open in Figma; redraw on 24x24 pixel grid; ensure all paths are aligned to the pixel grid; expand strokes; boolean union; export SVG via SVGR.

**Step 6 — Build a Gem ('Icon Designer')**: System prompt: 'You produce single-color UI icons on 24x24 pixel grid, stroke 1.5px rounded caps, geometric simplicity. Always output SVG when possible. Never: gradient, fill, decorative elements, photorealism.' Pin reference images from your locked style guide.

**Step 7 — Audit pass**: Drop all 50 final icons in Figma on one page. Look for: stroke weight inconsistency, corner radius drift, optical size differences (a circle should look the same visual weight as a square — circles need to be ~2-3% larger than squares to optically match).
workflow_steps: 1. **Audit needed icons (45 min)**: List every screen, every action, every status. Typical product: 30-60 unique icons. Group by semantic family (nav, action, status, file-type). 2. **Decide library/custom/hybrid (30 min)**: Default to hybrid — Phosphor or Lucide as primary; custom only for 5-10 brand-defining icons (tab bar + main CTA). 3. **Library coverage audit (60 min)**: Check each needed icon against the chosen library's name list. Flag misses. 4. **Style spec lock (60 min)**: Document stroke weight, size grid, corner radius, perspective, fill rules. **For library use, this means picking the variant — Phosphor Regular (1.5px stroke) or Phosphor Bold (2px) or Phosphor Duotone**. **For Lucide, lock the strokeWidth prop (1.5 default, 2 for stronger)**. 5. **Custom icon generation (1-2 hours per icon)**: For the 5-10 gaps, generate via Imagen 4 + Recraft; finish in Figma on 24x24 pixel grid. 6. **Naming convention (15 min)**: kebab-case, semantic over visual (`heart` not `like`; allows changing meaning later). 7. **React/RN component generation**: SVGR CLI converts SVG → React component; or use Phosphor/Lucide imports directly. 8. **Storybook entry**: one screen with all icons in a grid for visual audit. 9. **Optical correction pass (60 min)**: zoom to 800%, walk through each icon, ensure pixel-grid alignment and optical balance. 10. **Document for AI/contributors**: a README with the spec so future you doesn't drift. **Total**: 8-15 hours for first system; ~30 min per icon added later.
ai_prompt_templates:
**Template 1 — Custom UI icon (stroke style)**: 'Single UI icon depicting {concept}, 24x24 pixel grid, 1.5px stroke weight, rounded line caps, single black color, transparent background, geometric simplicity, all paths aligned to pixel grid, balanced negative space, no fill, no gradient, no shadow, centered with 2px safe margin from canvas edges, in the style of {Phosphor Bold / Lucide / Heroicons outline}.'

**Template 2 — Custom UI icon (filled/duotone style)**: 'Single UI icon depicting {concept}, filled flat shapes in two tones {primary 100% opacity black + secondary 30% opacity black}, 24x24 grid, no stroke outline, geometric simplicity, optical balance, transparent background, in the style of Phosphor Duotone weight.'

**Template 3 — Brand-tab icon (slightly more detailed)**: 'Hero tab-bar icon, 32x32 pixel grid, two-color flat illustration depicting {concept metaphor}, archetype-aligned with {Magician/Creator}, slightly more detailed than utility icon but still geometric, color palette {hex tokens}, no gradient mesh, no 3D render, transparent background.'

**Template 4 — Recraft SVG conversion**: 'Convert this raster icon to a flat vector SVG, single 1.5px stroke, no fill, 24x24 viewBox, aligned to pixel grid, paths simplified, single layer.'

**Template 5 — Audit prompt for Gemini**: 'Compare these {N} icons. Identify inconsistencies in: stroke weight, corner radius, level of detail, perspective convention, optical balance. Output a numbered list of problems with the icon name.'
claude_code_integration: Install primary library: `pnpm add lucide-react-native` (or `phosphor-react-native`). Build `apps/mobile/src/components/Icon.tsx` — a single wrapper that takes a `name` prop and resolves to either the library icon or a custom one: `const ICON_MAP = { ...LUCIDE_ICONS, search: SearchIcon, /* custom */ logo: LogoIcon }`. This abstraction means swapping libraries later is one file edit. For custom icons, drop SVGs in `apps/mobile/src/icons/svg/`, run `npx @svgr/cli --typescript --native --out-dir src/icons/components src/icons/svg` to generate React components. Add to the wrapper. Ensure your tsconfig has `compilerOptions.allowSyntheticDefaultImports`. For sizes: prop `size: 16 | 20 | 24 | 32` maps to a fixed enum — DO NOT allow arbitrary sizes (drift). For stroke: prop `weight: 'regular' | 'bold'` maps to library variant. Storybook entry: render the entire icon catalog on one screen for design review. Linting: add an ESLint rule blocking direct imports from `lucide-react-native` outside `Icon.tsx` (`no-restricted-imports`) so the team can't bypass the wrapper.
emotional_design_integration: **Magician/Creator**: Phosphor Duotone (warm, crafted) OR custom hand-drawn icons with intentional micro-imperfection (slight stroke variation, off-grid wobble). Anti-pattern: Heroicons (too generic, reads 'Tailwind default'). **Caregiver**: rounded corners (4px+), generous padding, slightly thicker stroke (1.75-2px), lower optical density. Phosphor Bold or custom rounded set. **Hero**: Lucide default, sharp 1.5px stroke, geometric, no decoration; precision = trust. **Jester**: custom playful set with slight tilts, asymmetries, occasional extra detail (a heart icon with a tiny crack). **Outlaw**: thicker stroke (2-2.5px), sharper corners, occasionally broken path, monochrome. **Innocent**: very rounded, pastel-on-white, soft duotone. **Sage**: thin stroke (1-1.25px), generous proportions, classical. **Numerical hierarchy alternative**: instead of icons in a settings menu, use `01.`, `02.`, `03.` prefix with monospace font — signals editorial confidence and works incredibly well for Magician/Creator/Sage archetypes. Notion does this in some menus; Things 3 uses it in its Today list.
specific_examples: 1. **Linear** (https://linear.app) — fully custom icon set, hand-drawn-feeling stroke, slight character imperfection; archetype = Magician/Creator. They invested heavily in custom because icons ARE their brand fingerprint. 2. **Vercel dashboard** (https://vercel.com) — uses Geist Icons, their open-source set; minimal monochrome stroke, pixel-perfect; archetype = Hero/Sage. 3. **Stripe** (https://stripe.com) — custom subtle icons + restrained use; many Stripe screens use ZERO icons, choosing typography hierarchy instead. 4. **Notion** (https://notion.so) — uses Lucide (or close variant) + emoji + numerical hierarchy + custom emoji-as-icon system for pages; mixed strategy proven at scale. 5. **Cursor** (https://cursor.sh) — Lucide-based, dev-tool minimal. 6. **Things 3** (Cultured Code) — custom thin-stroke icons that feel hand-drawn; archetype = Magician/Sage. 7. **Apple's own apps (iOS 17+)** — SF Symbols pervasive; the variable weight + optical size axes mean one icon font covers infinite contexts. 8. **Figma's own UI** — custom set, slightly thicker stroke, rounded; archetype = Creator. 9. **GitHub (octicons)** — custom open-source set tuned for code-context; medium stroke, slightly geometric. 10. **Phosphor showcase apps** — Cash App marketing uses Phosphor Duotone, Notion uses elements of Phosphor; consumer fintech and creative apps cluster around it.
anti_ai_slop_traps: **Tells specific to AI-generated UI icons**: (a) Inconsistent stroke weight across the set — AI doesn't enforce a system; one icon at 1.2px, next at 1.8px. (b) Off-pixel-grid paths — AI vectors land on fractional pixels, causing blurry rendering at small sizes. (c) Unequal optical balance — AI doesn't know that a circle needs to be ~2.7% larger than a square to feel the same weight. (d) Mixed metaphor families — AI generates a 'house' for home but uses outline style and a 'gear' for settings filled style. (e) Decorative flourishes added by AI 'helpfulness' — sparkles around a star, motion lines on an arrow. (f) Six tiny dots clustered (AI's 'detail' default for menu/more icons). (g) Photorealistic 3D-ish shading on flat icons. **Library-as-slop signature**: (h) Heroicons everywhere shouts 'I used Tailwind starter and didn't customize.' (i) Material Icons in a non-Google-context shouts 'I used Bootstrap defaults.' (j) Mixed libraries — Heroicons + Lucide + emoji in one screen = visual chaos. **Counter-prompts**: 'aligned to pixel grid', '1.5px stroke uniform across set', 'no decorative flourish', 'no shading', 'matching style of {reference}'. **Counter-process**: ALWAYS pick ONE library or custom system and enforce it. Run a Storybook audit — print 50 icons on one page. The eye instantly catches inconsistencies. For custom: zoom to 800% in Figma and verify every path lands on integer pixels.
common_pitfalls: Mixing libraries — the #1 amateur signal. Picking Heroicons just because it's the Tailwind default — your competitors all did the same; you'll look generic. Choosing a library missing 5-10 needed icons and silently substituting odd matches (a 'compass' for 'navigate'). Inconsistent sizing — using 16, 18, 20, 24 randomly instead of a fixed 16/20/24 ladder. Forgetting RTL flip — arrows, back/forward, and play icons need RTL variants. Forgetting filled-vs-outline state convention — many designs use outline for default, filled for active; if not enforced, looks broken. Accessibility — clickable icons without labels are unusable for screen readers. Bundle bloat — importing the entire icon library instead of tree-shaking individual icons. Stroke-width prop drift — letting devs pass arbitrary `strokeWidth` instead of a fixed enum. Custom icons without a pixel-grid spec — the set drifts visually within months. Not building dark-mode variants — black icons on dark UI invisible.
accessibility_notes: Every icon button must have an accessible label. RN: `accessibilityLabel='Search'` on `<TouchableOpacity>`; web: `aria-label='Search'` on `<button>` or visually-hidden text. Decorative icons (next to a label) should be hidden from AT: `accessibilityElementsHidden={true}` (RN) or `aria-hidden='true'` (web). Color contrast: stroke must meet 3:1 against background per WCAG 2.2. Don't rely on icon alone to convey meaning — pair with text label for primary actions on the first encounter (especially for novel metaphors). Hit target: minimum 44x44pt on iOS, 48x48dp on Android — even if the icon is 20x20, the wrapping pressable area must be 44+. RTL languages need flipped directional icons (back arrow, forward, play). Reduced motion: animated icons (loading spinner) should have a static fallback or respect `prefers-reduced-motion`.
mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile: react-native-svg renders SVG well, but performance suffers with 50+ SVGs on screen (e.g., long list with per-row icons). Use react-native-vector-icons (icon font) or pre-rasterized PNG for performance-critical lists. SF Symbols available natively on iOS via `expo-symbols`; brilliant for system-feel apps but breaks visual consistency with Android/web. Web: SVG-as-React-component is the cleanest; sprite sheet fallback for static sites. Tree-shake aggressively (Lucide and Phosphor support per-icon imports). Both: stroke-width-based libraries scale better than raster — at 16px and 32px, vector retains sharpness; raster needs 1x/2x/3x assets. Web-only: CSS `currentColor` lets icons inherit text color; mobile needs explicit `color` prop. Mobile-only: iOS dynamic type / Android font scale should affect icon size; provide a `useScaledIconSize` hook.
